metadata.title: Why Chemistry's Orbitals Aren't the Physicist's

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The familiar p-x and p-y dumbbells are not eigenstates of L-z. Each is a coherent blend of two complex p states carrying opposite L-z projections. Same p subspace, different basis.

props.command: How do the real chemistry p orbitals relate to definite-Lz spherical harmonics?
props.segment: Why Chemistry's Orbitals Aren't the Physicist's
output: Both bases span the same l=1 space.
Real dumbbells trade sharp Lz for orientation.

beats.narration_text: For orbital angular momentum l equals one, there are three states: m equals minus one, zero, and plus one. Every linear combination remains inside the same three-dimensional p subspace and still has definite L squared.
The m equals plus one and minus one states are complex. Their phases wind in opposite directions around the z axis. Measuring L-z in either one gives a definite result: plus h-bar or minus h-bar.

Their probability densities are the same torus-shaped angular pattern around z. The opposite angular momentum is encoded in phase winding, not in two visibly different density shapes. A density plot hides that crucial distinction.

Add and subtract the opposite-m states with the proper relative phases. The azimuthal phase factors interfere into real angular functions. One combination points along x and another along y: the chemistry p-x and p-y dumbbells.

A real p-x orbital has two lobes separated by the y-z nodal plane. The signs on the lobes describe wavefunction phase, not positive and negative charge. Rotate the basis within the degenerate p space and the dumbbell orientation rotates.

The real dumbbell is not an L-z eigenstate. Its L-z expectation is zero, but zero expectation does not mean zero angular momentum. An L-z measurement gives plus h-bar or minus h-bar with equal probability.

Those two outcomes give L-z variance h-bar squared. Meanwhile L squared remains sharp at l times l plus one h-bar squared, which is two h-bar squared. The real orbital keeps definite total orbital angular momentum.

Neither basis is more real. The complex m basis diagonalizes L-z. The real Cartesian basis makes spatial orientation and bonding symmetry easy to see. They are unitary coordinate choices within one degenerate subspace.

Chemistry favors oriented orbitals because molecular axes and bonds break spherical symmetry. Physics often favors m states when a z-directed magnetic field or rotational symmetry makes L-z the convenient observable. Context chooses the basis.

The slogan needs one correction. Chemistry's p orbitals are still perfectly valid physicists' states. They are eigenstates of L squared, just not of L-z. And in many-electron chemistry, an orbital is often an approximate model object rather than an exact many-body state.

Prepare p-x and measure L-z. Half the trials return plus h-bar and half return minus h-bar. The average is zero, yet every individual result is nonzero. That is the hidden opposite-m content of the dumbbell.

Your turn. For p-y, what are the possible L-z outcomes? Again plus and minus h-bar, each with probability one half. What changes from p-x is the relative phase between those components, which rotates the dumbbell by ninety degrees.

Why do chemistry orbitals look different? Opposite-m phase eigenstates combine into real, oriented dumbbells. Total L squared stays sharp; L-z does not. Same l equals one subspace, different basis chosen for different questions.
Why Chemistry's Orbitals Aren't the Physicist's. Liam, in for Bear.
